metadata.title: The Brand Attribution Problem — Why a Metric Moving Proves Nothing
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how Claude synthesizes three documented attribution-problem cases and derives a five-question attribution checklist for brand measurement claims.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 13 — Measuring Brand Equity (Nina Harris)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: a number that moved is not a verdict. It is a question. And the question — did our brand work cause this? — almost always has an honest answer of: we cannot tell. We are building the five-question checklist that separates a metric moving from the work causing it.

props.command: Research three cases where correlation between brand investment and performance movement was claimed as causation — explain the confound in each. Derive a five-question attribution checklist any brand team should answer before attributing movement to brand work.
output: Case 1: NPS +3pts — competitor withdrew same quarter

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without an attribution checklist. You run a campaign. NPS goes up three points. The team declares success. The case study is written. The agency is renewed. Six months later: a competitor had pulled back distribution in the same quarter. The NPS movement was real. The cause was not what the team thought. The instinct to find one number and declare it the verdict is precisely what the attribution problem exploits.

props.sparkLine: The instinct to find one number is what the problem exploits.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter Thirteen. The attribution problem in brand measurement: correlation between brand investment and performance movement is not causation, and the alternative explanation is almost always present. Three structural confounds that appear repeatedly: competitor activity changing simultaneously, pricing or distribution changing in the same period, and seasonality or category tailwinds that lift all boats. The Pepsi Kendall Jenner case names the failure mode: awareness went up and favorability went down simultaneously — any single number extracted from a multidimensional brand asset answers one question while leaving the others open.

The centerpiece: five-question attribution checklist that Claude derives from the three case analyses. Question one: did the metric move before the campaign had time to cause it? Question two: was there a control group or holdout region? Question three: did competitor activity, pricing, or seasonality change simultaneously? Question four: can you account for the base rate of this metric improving on its own? Question five: would a holdout test have been possible, and if not, why not? These five questions separate a metric moving from the work causing it. None of them prove causation — they prove that the claim has survived scrutiny. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Thirteen.

Honesty about what the checklist does not do. Absence of causal proof does not mean brand work has no effect. The checklist is a discipline for making honest claims, not an argument that brand investment is ineffective. Some effects are real and genuinely caused by the brand work — the checklist helps name them accurately rather than over-claim them.

artifactLines: Absence of causal proof does not mean brand work is ineffective.
The checklist disciplines claims — it does not refute brand investment.
Some effects are real and caused by the work — name them accurately, not over-claimed.
The checklist is applied to specific claimed successes, not to brand strategy in general.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. The Brand Attribution Problem. Four lines.

artifactLines: A metric moving is a question. The attribution checklist is what turns it into a verdict.
Three structural confounds appear in almost every claimed success: competitor, pricing, seasonality.
Five questions separate correlation from causation — none prove it, all discipline the claim.
The Pepsi/Kendall Jenner case: awareness up, favorability down — same campaign, same quarter.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: I am going to describe my brand's most recent claimed success — a campaign or investment where a performance metric improved and we attributed it to our work. Here is what happened: describe your campaign, the metric that moved, and what your team attributed it to. Now run the five-question attribution checklist against this specific case: did the metric move before the campaign could have caused it? Was there a control group or holdout? Did competitor activity, pricing, or seasonality change simultaneously? Can you account for the base rate? Would a holdout test have been possible? For each question, tell me whether we can answer it with the data we have and what additional information would be needed. Be honest. The checklist is not accusing you of bad work — it is naming what you would need to know to defend the claim under scrutiny.

props.command: Run the five-question attribution checklist on my brand's claimed success: [CAMPAIGN + METRIC + CLAIMED CAUSE]. For each question, can we answer it with current data? What additional information would be needed to defend the claim?

beats.narration_text: The Brand Attribution Problem — Why a Metric Moving Proves Nothing. Liam, in for Bear.
